literal: thought-organ; from √man 'to think' (cognate with Latin *mens*, Greek *menos*, English 'mind')

cognates.note: True cognate: Skt. manas and Greek menos are exact formal matches (PIE *men-os), with Latin mēns and English ‘mind’ from the same root.

invariant_definition: *Manas* is the coordinating and oscillating inner organ that gathers sensory data, attends, compares, doubts, imagines, and turns toward one option rather than another. It is therefore not equivalent to the whole of 'mind'. In the standard fourfold analysis, *manas* presents and wavers, *buddhi* determines, *ahaṅkāra* appropriates, and *citta* retains. Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika sharpens one side of this picture by treating *manas* as an atomic internal sense whose serial contact explains why cognitions occur one at a time; Yogācāra repurposes the term for a self-grasping consciousness in its own way. Vedānta usually preserves the Sāṅkhya-style functional analysis and relies on *manas* to explain distraction, indecision, fantasy, and the need for meditative restraint. In practice, instruction aimed at concentration and recollection works first on *manas*, because it is where the senses are gathered or scattered and where an intention is either stabilized or lost.

school_framing.shared_core: *Manas* names **the sense-coordinating cognitive function: the inner sense (*antar-indriya*) that synthesizes discrete sense-data and presents them to *buddhi***. *Sāṅkhya-Kārikā* 27 fixes *manas* as the *saṅkalpaka* function. *Nyāya-Sūtra* 1.1.16 treats it as an atomic internal sense. All Brāhmaṇical schools share the structural role.
school_framing.register_axes_note: Same Φ+E register, same scope-inside. Real disagreement only at the M-level: Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats *manas* as *aṇu* (atomic), Sāṅkhya as a *prakṛti*-evolute, Advaita as an *upādhi*-mode. Methodology AF1 holds on the *aṇu* question between Nyāya and Sāṅkhya.

per_school.definition: In Advaita, *manas* is a vṛtti-bearing instrument rather than self-luminous consciousness: Madhusūdana defines *bhakti* as the mind's assuming Bhagavat's form and *brahma-vidyā* as a distinct *mano-vṛtti* directed to the non-dual self. [cite://madhusudana/bhakti-rasayana/1.ṭīkā.1] Advaita therefore treats *manas* as part of the inner apparatus from which the self must be discriminated, since body, senses, and mind alike can appear under the appropriation of the "I"-cognition. [cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/4] Its restraint as *śama* is accordingly defined as the settled abiding of *manas* in its proper aim after repeated dispassion toward objects. [cite://sankara/vivekacudamani/22]

Viśiṣṭādvaita distinguishes the self from body, senses, *manas*, vital breaths, and cognitions; *manas* accordingly belongs to the embodied apparatus rather than to the *jīva*'s essential nature. [cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/3] When karmically produced differentiations are removed, the self remains *jñāna-svarūpa*; mental fluctuation is therefore adventitious to saṃsāric embodiment and not constitutive of the self's own essence. [cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§5]

Madhva counts *manas* among the dependent tattvas together with *buddhi*, the ten senses, the subtle elements, and the gross elements. [cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/9] He also preserves the Kaṭha hierarchy in which *manas* stands above the senses and their objects but below *buddhi*; it is therefore a real internal instrument and not the conscious self itself. [cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/Upaniṣad 1.3.10, cite://madhva/tattva-sankhyana/9]
primary_loci: Kaṭha Upaniṣad 1.3.10 as cited in Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana

per_school.definition: In classical Sāṅkhya, *manas* is *ubhayātmaka*: it counts as an *indriya* and its distinctive function is *saṃkalpa*. [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/27] Because it stands in the same sāttvika line as the other faculties, it mediates both cognitive and active operations without being the determining *buddhi* or the witnessing *puruṣa*. [cite://isvara-krsna/sankhya-karika/27]

Yoga treats *manas* under the discipline of stabilization: an arisen objective cognition may become *manasaḥ sthitinibandhanī*, a binding of the mind to steadiness. [cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.35] Prāṇāyāma and the subsequent limbs make *manas* fit for *dhāraṇā*, so yogic practice works directly on the mind's steadiness and availability for concentration. [cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.53]

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats *manas* as an internal sense serving the apprehension of pleasure and the rest, one for each self, eternal, and atomic. [cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/18, cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.23] Its existence is inferred from the non-simultaneity of cognitions, and precisely that seriality grounds the theses that there is only one *manas* per self and that it is minute rather than pervasive. [cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.16, cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/3.2.56-59, cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/3.2.1-3]

In the Bhāṭṭa Mīmāṃsā discussion of *vyāpti*, Kumārila rejects the proposal that pervasion is grasped by *manas* independently, insisting that mind is not autonomous in cognition of external objects. [cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14] The denial is principled: if *manas* were externally autonomous, everyone would in effect perceive everything, so mental operation must remain conditioned by other epistemic factors rather than function as an all-reaching inner sense. [cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/3.14]

Nāgārjuna lists *manas* alongside seeing, hearing, smelling, tasting, and touching as the sixth *indriya*. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1] Because chapter 3 subjects these faculties and their objects alike to Madhyamaka analysis, *manas* is admitted only as a conventional sense-faculty and not as a self-established inner subject. [cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.1, cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/3.9]

Yogācāra gives *manas* a specialized role: Vasubandhu calls it the consciousness named *manas*, whose nature is *manana* and which proceeds taking the *ālaya* as its object. [cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/5] In the broader *vijñaptimātra* register, however, *citta*, *manas*, *vijñāna*, and *vijñapti* can be deployed as near-synonyms, so context determines whether the term names the seventh consciousness specifically or consciousness-language more generally. [cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/Proem]

In Abhinavagupta's Pratyabhijñā exegesis, *manas* is the locus of *saṃkalpa* when cognition is conditioned by an indistinct object, in contrast to *jñāna* through the external senses and *adhyavasāya* through *buddhi*. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/1.5.18] He accordingly counts *manas* within the threefold *antaḥkaraṇa* of the finite subject as the instrument of *saṃkalpa* and related operations, not as ultimate *cit* itself. [cite://abhinavagupta/ishvara-pratyabhijna-vimarshini/3.1.11]

Jainism incorporates mind into its taxonomy of cognition by distinguishing *mati-jñāna* as arising from sensory and non-sensory conditions and by recognizing *manaḥparyaya-jñāna* as a distinct form of knowledge. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-14, cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.23-29] The division of embodied beings into *samanaska* and *amanaska* shows that possession of mind is a differentiating feature within saṃsāra rather than the defining essence of *jīva* as such. [cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/2.11]

textual_history.summary: *Manas*, from √man 'to think' (an inherited Indo-European word: Latin *mens*, Greek *menos*, English 'mind'), is the oldest and most stable of the cluster — it is fully present already in the Ṛgveda, where it names the thinking-and-willing organ seated in the heart, famed above all for its *speed*: the gods' chariot is 'swifter than mind', and mind is 'the swiftest of flying things'. The Saṃhitā already locates desire and intention in *manas* — the cosmogonic 'desire that was the first seed of mind' (RV 10.129.4). The Upaniṣads place *manas* within an explicit hierarchy of faculties, below *buddhi* and above the senses (Kaṭha 1.3.10), and in the chariot allegory cast it as the *reins* that the intellect-charioteer holds (Kaṭha 1.3.3). Classical Sāṅkhya fixes its technical function as *saṅkalpa* — the synthesising, intending operation — and calls it *ubhayātmaka*, partaking of both the cognitive and active senses (SK 27). The *Gītā* inherits all of this and dramatises the practical problem: *manas* is *cañcala*, restless, and 'hard to restrain as the wind' (6.34–35), the very thing meditation must steady. The arc here is genuinely continuous — *manas* keeps the same core sense (the oscillating thought-organ) from Ṛgveda to Gītā; what changes is its placement in an ever more articulated psychology and the increasing emphasis on its restraint.

stages.sanskrit: dhruvaṁ jyotir nihitaṁ dṛśaye kam mano javiṣṭham patayatsv antaḥ | viśve devāḥ samanasaḥ saketā ekaṁ kratum abhi vi yanti sādhu
stages.rendering: A fixed light is set for seeing; mind (manas), the swiftest of things that fly, is within. All the gods, of one mind (sa-manas), of one intent, move rightly toward a single will (kratu).
stages.note: The Ṛgvedic *manas* in two of its defining aspects: its proverbial *swiftness* — 'the swiftest of flying things' — and its capacity to be *unified* (*sa-manas*, 'of one mind') and converge on a single *kratu* (will/intent). The mind-as-swift becomes a fixed Vedic topos (cf. the gods' chariot 'manojavas', e.g. RV 1.117.15). This is the seat of thought that the contemplative traditions will later strive to slow and steady; the Saṃhitā already treats its unification as an achievement.

stages.sanskrit: kāmas tad agre sam avartatādhi manaso retaḥ prathamaṁ yad āsīt | sato bandhum asati nir avindan hṛdi pratīṣyā kavayo manīṣā
stages.rendering: Desire (kāma) arose in the beginning upon that — which was the first seed of mind (manas); searching in the heart with wisdom (manīṣā), the seers found the bond of the existent in the non-existent.
stages.note: The creation-hymn locates the origin of differentiation in *manas* and *kāma*: desire is 'the first seed of mind'. Two motifs that persist appear here — *manas* as bound up with desire (which the Gītā's restless mind, full of *kāma*, will inherit), and cognition seated *in the heart* (*hṛdi … manīṣā*), the Vedic identification of the thinking organ with the heart. *Manas* is thus, from the start, the meeting-point of thought and wanting.

stages.sanskrit: buddhiṃ tu sārathiṃ viddhi manaḥ pragraham eva ca | indriyāṇi hayān āhur viṣayāṃs teṣu gocarān
stages.rendering: Know the intellect (buddhi) as the charioteer, and the mind (manas) as the reins; the senses, they say, are the horses, the sense-objects their range.
stages.note: In the chariot allegory *manas* is the *reins* (*pragraha*): the link through which the intellect-charioteer governs the senses-horses. The image fixes the standing contrast — *buddhi* determines and drives, *manas* transmits and conveys, the senses pull outward. *Manas* is here neither the self nor the ruling faculty but the indispensable connecting instrument that must be held firm; if loosened, the senses run wild (Kaṭha 1.3.5).

stages.sanskrit: indriyebhyaḥ parā hy arthā arthebhyaś ca paraṃ manaḥ | manasas tu parā buddhir buddher ātmā mahān paraḥ
stages.rendering: Higher than the senses are their objects; higher than the objects, the mind (manas); higher than the mind, the intellect (buddhi); higher than the intellect, the great self.
stages.note: The faculty-ladder fixes *manas* one rung above the senses and their objects, and one rung below *buddhi*. This is the precise placement the later *antaḥkaraṇa* psychology systematises: *manas* gathers and presents the sensory field, *buddhi* determines it. The Upaniṣad thus turns the Vedic thought-organ into a graded level of the inner apparatus — a level to be transcended on the way inward to the self.

stages.sanskrit: ubhayātmakam atra manaḥ saṅkalpakam indriyaṃ ca sādharmyāt | guṇapariṇāmaviśeṣān nānātvaṃ grāhyabhedāc ca
stages.rendering: Here the mind (manas) is of both natures: it is the synthesising/intending faculty (saṅkalpaka) and [also] a sense, by reason of its kinship [with both]. Its diversity [of operations] is from the particular transformations of the guṇas and from the difference of objects grasped.
stages.note: Sāṅkhya fixes the technical function: the distinctive work of *manas* is *saṅkalpa* — synthesising the raw sensory data and forming it into a determinate, intended presentation — and it is *ubhayātmaka*, counting on both the cognitive (*buddhīndriya*) and active (*karmendriya*) sides. This is the classical 'inner sense' that mediates between the outer senses and the determining *buddhi*; the Gītā's restless *manas* is this same coordinating organ viewed as a discipline-problem.

stages.sanskrit: cañcalaṃ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham | tasyāhaṃ nigrahaṃ manye vāyor iva suduṣkaram || … asaṃśayaṃ mahābāho mano durṇigrahaṃ calam | abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇaś ca gṛhyate
stages.rendering: For the mind (manas) is restless, Kṛṣṇa, turbulent, strong, and obstinate; its restraint I think as hard to achieve as the wind's. … 'Without doubt, mighty-armed one, the mind is hard to restrain and unsteady; yet by practice and by dispassion, Kaunteya, it is held.'
stages.note: The *Gītā* makes *manas* the central practical obstacle: Arjuna protests that the mind is *cañcala*, 'restless', and its control as hard as bottling the wind — and Kṛṣṇa concedes the difficulty but answers with *abhyāsa* (practice) and *vairāgya* (dispassion). The Vedic swift mind (stage 1) reappears here as the *problem* of practice: what was praised as the swiftest of flying things is now what must be slowed and gathered. The whole meditative programme of restraint converges on *manas*.

translator_note: 'Mind' is too broad and usually wrong if left unqualified. *Manas* is one function within a larger inner apparatus; it does not cover the whole mental life and certainly not pure consciousness. Translating it as 'mind' without noting the contrast with *buddhi*, *citta*, and *ahaṅkāra* imports a modern unitary notion of mind that the Sanskrit psychological models explicitly resist.
